turns.content_text:
We're currently solving the following issue within our repository. Here's the issue text:
ISSUE:
py2-py3 invalid escape sequence: '\N' should get rewritten
This is a syntax error in python3.x:

```python
'\N'
```

INSTRUCTIONS:
Now, you're going to solve this issue on your own. Your terminal session has started and you're in the repository's root directory. You can use any bash commands or the special interface to help you. Edit all the files you need to and run any checks or tests that you want.
Remember, YOU CAN ONLY ENTER ONE COMMAND AT A TIME. You should always wait for feedback after every command.
When you're satisfied with all of the changes you've made, you can submit your changes to the code base by simply running the submit command.
Note however that you cannot use any interactive session commands (e.g. python, vim) in this environment, but you can write scripts and run them. E.g. you can write a python script and then run it with `python <script_name>.py`.

NOTE ABOUT THE EDIT COMMAND: Indentation really matters! When editing a file, make sure to insert appropriate indentation before each line!

IMPORTANT TIPS:
1. Always start by trying to replicate the bug that the issues discusses.
   If the issue includes code for reproducing the bug, we recommend that you re-implement that in your environment, and run it to make sure you can reproduce the bug.
   Then start trying to fix it.
   When you think you've fixed the bug, re-run the bug reproduction script to make sure that the bug has indeed been fixed.

   If the bug reproduction script does not print anything when it successfully runs, we recommend adding a print("Script completed successfully, no errors.") command at the end of the file,
   so that you can be sure that the script indeed ran fine all the way through.

2. If you run a command and it doesn't work, try running a different command. A command that did not work once will not work the second time unless you modify it!

3. If you open a file and need to get to an area around a specific line that is not in the first 100 lines, say line 583, don't just use the scroll_down command multiple times. Instead, use the goto 583 command. It's much quicker.

4. If the bug reproduction script requires inputting/reading a specific file, such as buggy-input.png, and you'd like to understand how to input that file, conduct a search in the existing repo code, to see whether someone else has already done that. Do this by running the command: find_file "buggy-input.png" If that doesn't work, use the linux 'find' command.

5. Always make sure to look at the currently open file and the current working directory (which appears right after the currently open file). The currently open file might be in a different directory than the working directory! Note that some commands, such as 'create', open files, so they might change the current  open file.

6. When editing files, it is easy to accidentally specify a wrong line number or to write code with incorrect indentation. Always check the code after you issue an edit to make sure that it reflects what you wanted to accomplish. If it didn't, issue another command to fix it.

bash-$

The issue is that the `\N` escape sequence is not valid in Python 3.x. To fix this, we need to find where the `\N` escape sequence is used in the codebase and replace it with the correct Unicode character. First, let's search for the `\N` escape sequence in the repository to identify where it is used.
```
search_dir '\N'
```

Found 163 matches for "\N" in /pyupgrade:
/pyupgrade/LICENSE (6 matches)
/pyupgrade/README.md (2 matches)
/pyupgrade/pyupgrade.egg-info/PKG-INFO (4 matches)
/pyupgrade/pyupgrade.egg-info/SOURCES.txt (2 matches)
/pyupgrade/pyupgrade.py (110 matches)
/pyupgrade/setup.cfg (1 matches)
/pyupgrade/tests/pyupgrade_test.py (38 matches)
End of matches for "\N" in /pyupgrade

open pyupgrade.py

27:def parse_format(s):
28:    """Makes the empty string not a special case.  In the stdlib, there's
29:    loss of information (the type) on the empty string.
30:    """
31:    parsed = tuple(_stdlib_parse_format(s))

33:        return ((s, None, None, None),)

38:def unparse_parsed_string(parsed):
39:    stype = type(parsed[0][0])
40:    j = stype()

42:    def _convert_tup(tup):
43:        ret, field_name, format_spec, conversion = tup
44:        ret = ret.replace(stype('{'), stype('{{'))
45:        ret = ret.replace(stype('}'), stype('}}'))
46:        if field_name is not None:
47:            ret += stype('{') + field_name
48:            if conversion:
49:                ret += stype('!') + conversion
50:            if format_spec:
51:                ret += stype(':') + format_spec
52:            ret += stype('}')
53:        return ret
54:
55:    return j.join(_convert_tup(tup) for tup in parsed)

92:            last_int += 1
93:        else:
94:            return literal

106:def _fix_format_literals(contents_text):
107:    tokens = src_to_tokens(contents_text)
108:
109:    to_replace = []
110:    string_start = None
111:    string_end = None

115:        if string_start is None and token.name == 'STRING':
116:            string_start = i
117:            string_end = i + 1
118:        elif string_start is not None and token.name == 'STRING':
119:            string_end = i + 1
120:        elif string_start is not None and token.src == '.':
121:            seen_dot = True
122:        elif seen_dot and token.src == 'format':
123:            to_replace.append((string_start, string_end))
124:            string_start, string_end, seen_dot = None, None, False
125:        elif token.name not in NON_CODING_TOKENS:
126:            string_start, string_end, seen_dot = None, None, False
127:
128:    for start, end in reversed(to_replace):
129:        src = tokens_to_src(tokens[start:end])
130:        new_src = _rewrite_string_literal(src)
131:        tokens[start:end] = [Token('STRING', new_src)]
132:
133:    return tokens_to_src(tokens)

206:Victims = collections.namedtuple(

211:def _victims(tokens, start, arg, gen):
212:    starts = [start]
213:    start_depths = [1]
214:    ends = []
215:    first_comma_index = None
216:    arg_depth = None
217:    arg_index = _arg_token_index(tokens, start, arg)
218:    brace_stack = [tokens[start].src]
219:    i = start + 1
220:
221:    while brace_stack:
222:        token = tokens[i].src
223:        is_start_brace = token in BRACES
224:        is_end_brace = token == BRACES[brace_stack[-1]]
225:
226:        if i == arg_index:
227:            arg_depth = len(brace_stack)
228:
229:        if is_start_brace:
230:            brace_stack.append(token)
231:
232:        # Remove all braces before the first element of the inner
233:        # comprehension's target.
234:        if is_start_brace and arg_depth is None:
235:            start_depths.append(len(brace_stack))
236:            starts.append(i)
237:
238:        if (
239:                token == ',' and
240:                len(brace_stack) == arg_depth and
241:                first_comma_index is None
242:        ):
243:            first_comma_index = i
244:
245:        if is_end_brace and len(brace_stack) in start_depths:
246:            if tokens[i - 2].src == ',' and tokens[i - 1].src == ' ':
247:                ends.extend((i - 2, i - 1, i))
248:            elif tokens[i - 1].src == ',':
249:                ends.extend((i - 1, i))
250:            else:
251:                ends.append(i)
252:
253:        if is_end_brace:
254:            brace_stack.pop()
255:
256:        i += 1
257:
258:    # May need to remove a trailing comma for a comprehension
259:    if gen:
260:        i -= 2
261:        while tokens[i].name in NON_CODING_TOKENS:
262:            i -= 1
263:        if tokens[i].src == ',':
264:            ends = sorted(set(ends + [i]))
265:
266:    return Victims(starts, ends, first_comma_index, arg_index)

401:def _fix_py2_compatible(contents_text):
402:    try:
403:        ast_obj = ast_parse(contents_text)
404:    except SyntaxError:
405:        return contents_text
406:    visitor = Py2CompatibleVisitor()
407:    visitor.visit(ast_obj)
408:    if not any((
409:            visitor.dicts,
410:            visitor.sets,
411:            visitor.set_empty_literals,
412:            visitor.is_literal,
413:    )):
414:        return contents_text
415:
416:    tokens = src_to_tokens(contents_text)
417:    for i, token in reversed_enumerate(tokens):
418:        if token.offset in visitor.dicts:
419:            _process_dict_comp(tokens, i, visitor.dicts[token.offset])
420:        elif token.offset in visitor.set_empty_literals:
421:            _process_set_empty_literal(tokens, i)
422:        elif token.offset in visitor.sets:
423:            _process_set_literal(tokens, i, visitor.sets[token.offset])
424:        elif token.offset in visitor.is_literal:
425:            _process_is_literal(tokens, i, visitor.is_literal[token.offset])
426:    return tokens_to_src(tokens)

429:def _imports_unicode_literals(contents_text):
430:    try:
431:        ast_obj = ast_parse(contents_text)
432:    except SyntaxError:
433:        return False
434:
435:    for node in ast_obj.body:
436:        # Docstring
437:        if isinstance(node, ast.Expr) and isinstance(node.value, ast.Str):
438:            continue
439:        elif isinstance(node, ast.ImportFrom):
440:            if (
441:                node.module == '__future__' and
442:                any(name.name == 'unicode_literals' for name in node.names)
443:            ):
444:                return True
445:            elif node.module == '__future__':
446:                continue
447:            else:
448:                return False
449:        else:
450:            return False
